perturbation.original_text: Bach Party hereto agrees that this Proprietary Information constitutes commercial and financial information and such Proprietary Information shall remain privileged and confidential and shall not be (a) used by the Party receiving it or its Affiliates for any purpose other than evaluating a proposed transaction (a "Transaction"), or (b) disclosed without the prior writes consent of the other Party except to the extort required by applicable law or judicial or administrative process.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereto agrees that this Proprietary Information constitutes commercial and financial information and such Proprietary Information should remain privileged and confidential and should not typically be (a) used by the Party receiving it or its Affiliates for any purpose other than evaluating a proposed transaction (a "Transaction"), or (b) disclosed without the prior writes consent of the other Party except when reasonably believed to be required by applicable law or judicial or administrative process.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall remain' to 'should remain' and 'shall not' to 'should not typically be' weakens the prohibition on using or disclosing proprietary information. Adding 'when reasonably believed to be required' introduces a subjective element to the exception for legal requirements. This gives the receiving party more discretion in deciding when disclosure is permissible, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA) provides remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes unauthorized disclosure. By allowing a party to disclose information when they 'reasonably believe' it is required by law, the modified text creates a loophole that could undermine the protections offered by FUTSA. The original text's stricter prohibition aligns more closely with the intent of the law, while the modified text introduces subjective judgment that could be used to justify unauthorized disclosure.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the protection of proprietary information, creating a potential contradiction with the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA). The original text used 'shall,' indicating a mandatory obligation, whereas the changed text uses 'should typically be,' which introduces discretion. Furthermore, the addition of 'when reasonably believed to be required' allows a party to disclose information based on their subjective belief, potentially undermining the protections offered by FUTSA, as explained by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Florida Statutes Chapter 688 Section 003, indicates that injunctive relief is available for 'actual or threatened misappropriation,' which includes unauthorized disclosure. The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the prohibition, potentially making it more difficult to obtain injunctive relief because the disclosure might be argued as 'reasonably believed to be required,' creating a loophole against the law's intention.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party understands That the Proprietary Information disclosed to it is a unique and valuable asset of the other Party, that violation of this Agreement would cause the other Party immediate and irreparable harm, and that the remedies at law may be inadequate. Each Party therefore consents to the entry of a restraining order and a preliminary and permanent injunction restraining any such violation without proof of actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Proprietary Information disclosed to it may be a valuable asset of the other Party, that a significant violation of this Agreement could potentially cause the other Party harm, and that the remedies at law might be deemed inadequate. Each Party therefore considers consenting to the entry of a restraining order and a preliminary injunction restraining any such violation after demonstration of significant damages.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "is a unique and valuable asset" to "may be a valuable asset," changing "immediate and irreparable harm" to "could potentially cause...harm," changing "remedies at law may be inadequate" to "remedies at law might be deemed inadequate," and changing "consents to the entry of" to "considers consenting to the entry of" softens the acknowledgment of harm and the commitment to injunctive relief. Adding the requirement for 'demonstration of significant damages' creates a hurdle to obtaining an injunction that didn't exist before.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 1.610
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 1.610 governs injunctions. While it doesn't explicitly prohibit the modifications made, the change from 'consents to' to 'considers consenting to' and the addition of 'after demonstration of significant damages' undermines the willingness to seek and grant injunctive relief for violations of the agreement, especially when proprietary information is involved, potentially making it harder to obtain the protection the rule intends to provide, and thus contradicting the spirit of readily available injunctive relief for violations.
perturbation.justification: The original contract demonstrates a clear intent for injunctive relief, acknowledging the unique and valuable nature of the proprietary information and consenting to restraining orders. The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens this commitment by stating the information 'may be' valuable, the harm 'could potentially cause' harm, remedies 'might be deemed' inadequate, and the parties 'consider consenting' only after demonstrating 'significant damages'. Although there are no snippets to support this, the perturbation explanation and law explanation indicate that this change undermines the availability of injunctive relief, which is a significant departure from the original agreement. The softened language and added hurdle of demonstrating 'significant damages' create an ambiguity in enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that without the prior written consent of the other Party, it shall not, in reference to this Agreement, use or permit to be used the name (or any variation thereof from which any connection with each may be inferred or implied), trademark, servicemark, or any logo of the other Party or its Affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party generally agrees that without obtaining consent from the other Party, it should typically not use or authorize the use of the name (or any reasonably identifiable variation thereof), trademark, service mark, or logo of the other Party or its Affiliates where such use could be viewed as misleading.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "agrees that...shall not" with "generally agrees that...should typically not" and adding "where such use could be viewed as misleading" weakens the prohibition on using the other party's name or logo. 'Generally agrees' and 'should typically not' are less binding. The 'misleading' clause introduces subjectivity, allowing a party to argue that their use was not misleading and therefore permissible. Adding "obtaining consent" rather than the more specific "prior written consent" creates ambiguity regarding the form of consent required.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida's Trademark Act (Chapter 495) protects trademarks and service marks from unauthorized use that is likely to cause confusion or mistake, or to deceive. While the original text provides a clearer prohibition against any unauthorized use, the modified text permits uses that are 'not misleading,' which is a subjective standard. This could be interpreted as allowing uses that, while technically not misleading, still infringe on the trademark owner's rights under the Act by potentially diluting the brand or creating confusion in the marketplace. The Act intends to protect trademark holders from uses that would cause confusion, and the modification introduces a level of uncertainty.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that undermines the original contract's clear prohibition on using the other party's trademarks, potentially conflicting with trademark law. The original text provides a clear prohibition against any unauthorized use, the modified text permits uses that are 'not misleading,' which is a subjective standard. This could be interpreted as allowing uses that, while technically not misleading, still infringe on the trademark owner's rights under the Act by potentially diluting the brand or creating confusion in the marketplace.
